passages.title: The History of Vaccination
passages.text:
Vaccination is one of the most effective public health interventions in history, saving millions of lives each year. The story of vaccination begins with a deadly disease that once terrorized humanity: smallpox.

The practice of variolation - deliberately infecting people with mild cases of smallpox to prevent severe disease - was used in China, India, and the Ottoman Empire for centuries before it reached Europe. Lady Mary Wortley Montagu observed this practice in Constantinople in 1717 and advocated for its use in England.

The true breakthrough came from Edward Jenner, an English country doctor. In 1796, Jenner observed that milkmaids who had contracted cowpox, a milder disease, seemed immune to smallpox. He tested his hypothesis by inoculating a young boy with cowpox material, then exposing him to smallpox. The boy did not develop smallpox. Jenner called his method 'vaccination,' from vacca, the Latin word for cow.

Louis Pasteur revolutionized vaccination in the late 19th century by developing methods to weaken, or attenuate, disease-causing microorganisms. He created vaccines for anthrax (1881) and rabies (1885), and his principles of attenuation laid the foundation for modern vaccine development. Pasteur's work established the germ theory of disease and demonstrated that vaccines could be created systematically rather than discovered by chance.

The 20th century saw remarkable progress. The BCG vaccine for tuberculosis was developed in 1921. The polio vaccine, developed by Jonas Salk in 1955 and Albert Sabin in 1961, brought one of the most feared diseases under control. The measles vaccine (1963) and the combined MMR vaccine (1971) dramatically reduced childhood mortality. Smallpox was declared eradicated in 1980 - the first and only human disease to be completely eliminated.

Vaccine technology continues to advance. The development of mRNA vaccines, which instruct cells to produce a harmless piece of the virus to trigger an immune response, represents a new frontier. The COVID-19 pandemic saw the first authorized use of mRNA vaccines, developed in record time. This technology holds promise for future vaccines against other infectious diseases and even cancer.

Despite their success, vaccines face challenges including misinformation, vaccine hesitancy, and unequal global access. Maintaining public trust in vaccines is essential for continued progress in global health.

questions.question: Variolation was practiced in China and India before reaching Europe.
questions.explanation: EN: 'The practice of variolation... was used in China, India, and the Ottoman Empire for centuries before it reached Europe.'|CN: '人痘接种术...在中国、印度和奥斯曼帝国使用了几个世纪后才传到欧洲。'

questions.question: Who advocated for the use of variolation in England in the 18th century?
questions.explanation: EN: 'Lady Mary Wortley Montagu observed this practice in Constantinople in 1717 and advocated for its use in England.'|CN: 'Mary Wortley Montagu夫人在1717年在君士坦丁堡观察到了这种做法，并倡导在英国使用。'

questions.question: Complete the sentence: Edward Jenner developed his vaccination method in _______.
questions.explanation: EN: 'In 1796, Jenner... tested his hypothesis by inoculating a young boy.'|CN: '1796年，Jenner...通过给一个小男孩接种来测试他的假设。'

questions.question: What Latin word does 'vaccination' come from, and what does it mean? (Word + meaning)
questions.explanation: EN: 'vaccination, from vacca, the Latin word for cow.'|CN: 'vaccination来自vacca，拉丁语中'牛'的意思。'

questions.question: Jenner observed that milkmaids who had cowpox were immune to smallpox.
questions.explanation: EN: 'Jenner observed that milkmaids who had contracted cowpox... seemed immune to smallpox.'|CN: 'Jenner观察到感染了牛痘的挤奶女工...似乎对天花免疫。'

questions.question: What diseases did Louis Pasteur create vaccines for?
questions.explanation: EN: 'He created vaccines for anthrax (1881) and rabies (1885).'|CN: '他创造了炭疽（1881年）和狂犬病（1885年）疫苗。'

questions.question: Complete the sentence: Pasteur's principles of _______ laid the foundation for modern vaccine development.
questions.explanation: EN: 'his principles of attenuation laid the foundation for modern vaccine development.'|CN: '他的弱化原理为现代疫苗开发奠定了基础。'

questions.question: Who developed the polio vaccine? (Two names with their years separated by 'and')
questions.explanation: EN: 'The polio vaccine, developed by Jonas Salk in 1955 and Albert Sabin in 1961.'|CN: '脊髓灰质炎疫苗，由Jonas Salk于1955年和Albert Sabin于1961年开发。'

questions.question: Smallpox was declared eradicated in 1980.
questions.explanation: EN: 'Smallpox was declared eradicated in 1980.'|CN: '天花于1980年被宣布消灭。'

questions.question: What is unique about mRNA vaccine technology?
options: B. It instructs cells to produce a harmless piece of virus
C. It only works for COVID-19
D. It was invented in the 1990s
questions.correctAnswer: B. It instructs cells to produce a harmless piece of virus
questions.explanation: EN: 'mRNA vaccines... instruct cells to produce a harmless piece of the virus to trigger an immune response.'|CN: 'mRNA疫苗...指示细胞产生无害的病毒片段以触发免疫反应。'

questions.question: mRNA vaccines were first authorized for use during the COVID-19 pandemic.
questions.explanation: EN: 'The COVID-19 pandemic saw the first authorized use of mRNA vaccines.'|CN: 'COVID-19大流行期间见证了mRNA疫苗的首次获批使用。'

questions.question: Complete the sentence: Vaccines face challenges including misinformation, vaccine hesitancy, and unequal _______.
questions.explanation: EN: 'vaccines face challenges including misinformation, vaccine hesitancy, and unequal global access.'|CN: '疫苗面临包括错误信息、疫苗犹豫和不公平的全球获取在内的挑战。'

questions.question: The combined MMR vaccine was developed in what year? (Four-digit number)
questions.explanation: EN: 'the combined MMR vaccine (1971).'|CN: '联合MMR疫苗（1971年）。'

passages.title: The Psychology of Consumer Behavior
passages.text:
Understanding why people buy what they buy is the central concern of consumer psychology. This field draws on cognitive psychology, social psychology, and economics to explain and predict consumer choices.

One of the most influential frameworks is the elaboration likelihood model, which describes two routes to persuasion. The central route involves careful, thoughtful consideration of arguments and evidence. Consumers taking this route are influenced by the quality of information. The peripheral route relies on superficial cues like attractive packaging, celebrity endorsements, or emotional appeals. Marketers must understand which route their target audience is likely to take.

Social proof is a powerful driver of consumer behavior. People tend to follow the actions of others, particularly in uncertain situations. This is why customer reviews, testimonials, and user numbers are prominently displayed on e-commerce sites. The principle of scarcity also influences behavior - when products are perceived as rare or limited, they become more desirable.

The endowment effect describes how people value things more highly simply because they own them. This is why free trials are effective - once consumers have a product, they are reluctant to give it up. Related to this is the status quo bias, where people prefer things to stay the same rather than change.

Price perception is more complex than economics textbooks suggest. The anchoring effect means that the first price a consumer sees becomes a reference point for evaluating subsequent prices. This is why retailers often display a higher original price crossed out next to the sale price. The charm pricing effect - prices ending in .99 or .95 - continues to be effective, though consumers are increasingly aware of this tactic.

Digital technology has transformed consumer behavior. Personalized recommendations based on browsing history, targeted advertising, and one-click purchasing reduce friction in the buying process. However, the paradox of choice suggests that too many options can lead to decision paralysis and reduced satisfaction. This is why successful online retailers often curate selections rather than overwhelming consumers with every possible choice.

Brand loyalty is another key concept. Brands create emotional connections with consumers through consistent quality, shared values, and identity signaling. Consumers often choose brands that reflect their self-image or the image they want to project. Building brand loyalty is more cost-effective than constantly acquiring new customers, which is why companies invest heavily in customer retention programs.

questions.question: What does the elaboration likelihood model describe?
options: A. How to create effective advertisements
C. The stages of brand loyalty
questions.explanation: EN: 'the elaboration likelihood model... describes two routes to persuasion.'|CN: '详尽可能性模型描述了说服的两条路径。'

questions.question: What type of route to persuasion involves careful consideration of arguments? (One word answer)
questions.explanation: EN: 'The central route involves careful, thoughtful consideration of arguments and evidence.'|CN: '中心路径涉及对论据和证据的仔细、深思熟虑的考虑。'

questions.question: Complete the sentence: Social proof means people tend to follow the actions of _______, particularly in uncertain situations.
questions.explanation: EN: 'People tend to follow the actions of others, particularly in uncertain situations.'|CN: '人们倾向于跟随他人的行为，特别是在不确定的情况下。'

questions.question: Customer reviews and testimonials are examples of social proof.
questions.explanation: EN: 'customer reviews, testimonials, and user numbers are prominently displayed on e-commerce sites' as social proof.|CN: '客户评价、推荐信和用户数量显著展示在电子商务网站上'作为社会证明。

questions.question: What does the endowment effect describe?
options: B. People value things more because they own them
C. People want to end old product lines
questions.correctAnswer: B. People value things more because they own them
questions.explanation: EN: 'The endowment effect describes how people value things more highly simply because they own them.'|CN: '禀赋效应描述了人们仅仅因为拥有某物而更加重视它。'

questions.question: Complete the sentence: The status quo bias means people prefer things to _______ rather than change.
questions.explanation: EN: 'the status quo bias, where people prefer things to stay the same rather than change.'|CN: '现状偏见，人们希望事物保持不变而不是改变。'

questions.question: Charm pricing (prices ending in .99) is no longer effective with modern consumers.
questions.explanation: EN: 'The charm pricing effect... continues to be effective, though consumers are increasingly aware of this tactic.'|CN: '魅力定价效应...仍然有效，尽管消费者对这种策略越来越了解。'

questions.question: What phenomenon describes too many choices leading to decision paralysis? (Three-word answer)
questions.explanation: EN: 'the paradox of choice suggests that too many options can lead to decision paralysis.'|CN: '选择的悖论表明过多的选择会导致决策瘫痪。'

questions.question: Successful online retailers often offer every possible choice to consumers.
questions.explanation: EN: 'successful online retailers often curate selections rather than overwhelming consumers with every possible choice.'|CN: '成功的在线零售商经常策展选择，而不是用所有可能的选择淹没消费者。'

questions.question: According to the passage, why do consumers often choose brands?
options: B. Because brands reflect their self-image
C. Because there are no alternatives
questions.correctAnswer: B. Because brands reflect their self-image
questions.explanation: EN: 'Consumers often choose brands that reflect their self-image or the image they want to project.'|CN: '消费者经常选择反映自我形象或他们想要展示的形象的品牌。'

questions.question: Complete the sentence: Building brand loyalty is more cost-effective than constantly _______ new customers.
questions.explanation: EN: 'Building brand loyalty is more cost-effective than constantly acquiring new customers.'|CN: '建立品牌忠诚度比不断获取新客户更具成本效益。'

questions.question: The author states that price perception is simple and straightforward.
questions.explanation: EN: 'Price perception is more complex than economics textbooks suggest.'|CN: '价格感知比经济学教科书中暗示的更复杂。'

questions.question: The anchoring effect in pricing means the first price a consumer sees becomes a what? (Three-word answer)
questions.explanation: EN: 'the first price a consumer sees becomes a reference point for evaluating subsequent prices.'|CN: '消费者看到的第一个价格成为评估后续价格的参考点。'

passages.title: Artificial Intelligence and Ethics
passages.text:
Artificial intelligence has made remarkable progress in recent years, achieving superhuman performance in games like Go and chess, powering autonomous vehicles, and enabling breakthroughs in medical diagnosis. As AI systems become more powerful and pervasive, the ethical implications of their deployment have become a subject of urgent debate.

One fundamental ethical concern is bias in AI systems. Machine learning models trained on historical data can inherit and amplify existing societal biases. For example, facial recognition systems have been shown to have lower accuracy for people with darker skin and women. Hiring algorithms trained on past hiring data may perpetuate gender or racial discrimination. Addressing these biases requires careful attention to training data, algorithmic transparency, and ongoing monitoring.

Accountability is another critical issue. When an autonomous vehicle causes an accident, who is responsible - the manufacturer, the software developer, the vehicle owner, or the AI itself? Current legal frameworks were not designed for such questions. The concept of meaningful human control suggests that humans should retain ultimate authority over AI systems, particularly in high-stakes decisions.

Privacy concerns are amplified by AI. Facial recognition, behavioral tracking, and data mining enable unprecedented surveillance capabilities. The collection and analysis of personal data by both governments and corporations raise questions about consent, data security, and the boundary between public safety and individual privacy.

The impact of AI on employment is a significant concern. While AI creates new jobs and can augment human capabilities, it also automates many existing roles. The World Economic Forum estimates that AI could displace 85 million jobs by 2025 but create 97 million new ones. Managing this transition requires investment in education, retraining programs, and social safety nets.

Transparency and explainability are increasingly demanded. Many AI systems, particularly deep learning models, are 'black boxes' - their decision-making processes are opaque even to their developers. Regulations like the EU's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) include provisions for a 'right to explanation' for algorithmic decisions affecting individuals.

The development of artificial general intelligence (AGI) - AI that matches or exceeds human capabilities across all domains - raises even deeper questions. Organizations like OpenAI and DeepMind have called for responsible development practices, safety research, and international cooperation to ensure that AGI benefits all of humanity.

questions.question: What is the main topic of this passage?
options: B. Ethical implications of AI deployment
C. The history of AI development
D. Comparing AI to human intelligence
questions.correctAnswer: B. Ethical implications of AI deployment
questions.explanation: EN: The passage discusses 'the ethical implications of their deployment' as a subject of urgent debate.|CN: 文章讨论了'AI部署的伦理影响'作为紧急辩论的主题。

questions.question: AI systems can inherit and amplify existing societal biases from training data.
questions.explanation: EN: 'Machine learning models trained on historical data can inherit and amplify existing societal biases.'|CN: '基于历史数据训练的机器学习模型可以继承并放大现有的社会偏见。'

questions.question: Complete the sentence: Facial recognition systems have lower accuracy for people with darker skin and _______.
questions.explanation: EN: 'facial recognition systems have been shown to have lower accuracy for people with darker skin and women.'|CN: '面部识别系统已被证明对肤色较深的人和女性准确性较低。'

questions.question: What concept suggests that humans should retain ultimate authority over AI systems? (Three-word answer)
questions.explanation: EN: 'The concept of meaningful human control suggests that humans should retain ultimate authority over AI systems.'|CN: '有意义的人类控制的概念表明人类应保留对AI系统的最终权力。'

questions.question: According to the World Economic Forum estimate, how many jobs could AI create by 2025?
questions.explanation: EN: 'AI could displace 85 million jobs by 2025 but create 97 million new ones.'|CN: 'AI到2025年可能取代8500万个工作岗位，但将创造9700万个新岗位。'

questions.question: Deep learning models are considered 'black boxes' because their decision-making is opaque.
questions.explanation: EN: 'Many AI systems, particularly deep learning models, are black boxes - their decision-making processes are opaque.'|CN: '许多AI系统，特别是深度学习模型，是黑盒子——它们的决策过程是不透明的。'

questions.question: Complete the sentence: The EU's GDPR includes provisions for a 'right to _______' for algorithmic decisions.
questions.explanation: EN: 'a right to explanation for algorithmic decisions affecting individuals.'|CN: '对影响个人的算法决策的解释权。'

questions.question: What does AGI stand for? (Full name)
questions.explanation: EN: 'artificial general intelligence (AGI) - AI that matches or exceeds human capabilities across all domains.'|CN: '人工通用智能（AGI）——在所有领域达到或超过人类能力的AI。'

questions.question: Facial recognition privacy concerns are only relevant for government use.
questions.explanation: EN: 'The collection and analysis of personal data by both governments and corporations' raises concerns.|CN: '政府和企业收集和分析个人数据'都引起了担忧。

questions.question: Complete the sentence: Managing the employment transition caused by AI requires investment in education, retraining programs, and _______.
questions.explanation: EN: 'Managing this transition requires investment in education, retraining programs, and social safety nets.'|CN: '管理这种过渡需要对教育、再培训计划和社会安全网进行投资。'

questions.question: Which organizations are mentioned as calling for responsible AI development practices?
questions.explanation: EN: 'Organizations like OpenAI and DeepMind have called for responsible development practices.'|CN: '像OpenAI和DeepMind这样的组织已经呼吁负责任的开发实践。'

questions.question: Current legal frameworks adequately address accountability issues with autonomous vehicles.
questions.explanation: EN: 'Current legal frameworks were not designed for such questions.'|CN: '当前的法律框架不是为这样的问题而设计的。'

questions.question: Complete the sentence: Addressing AI bias requires careful attention to training data, algorithmic transparency, and ongoing _______.
questions.explanation: EN: 'Addressing these biases requires careful attention to training data, algorithmic transparency, and ongoing monitoring.'|CN: '解决这些偏差需要关注训练数据、算法透明度和持续监控。'

questions.question: What does the author say has become a subject of urgent debate as AI becomes more powerful? (Three-word answer)
questions.explanation: EN: 'the ethical implications of their deployment have become a subject of urgent debate.'|CN: 'AI部署的伦理影响已成为紧急辩论的主题。'
